name: multi-stage
context: Multi-stage Dockerfile builds for smaller, secure production images by separating build and runtime layers
info: Multi-stage builds use multiple FROM statements in a single Dockerfile to separate build-time dependencies (compilers, dev tools, test frameworks) from the final runtime image. Only artifacts explicitly copied with COPY --from survive into the production stage. This technique reduces image size by 50-90%, shrinks the attack surface by eliminating build tools, and keeps secrets out of the final image. Named stages allow targeted builds with --target for development, testing, and production from a single Dockerfile. BuildKit executes independent stages in parallel for faster builds. Common patterns include builder-runner, test-then-deploy, and multi-language pipelines. This sub-skill focuses on multi-stage patterns and optimization; see [[docker/images/build]] for general Dockerfile authoring.
triggers: separate build and runtime in docker
body:
# Multi-Stage Docker Builds

Multi-stage builds use multiple `FROM` statements in a single Dockerfile to separate build-time dependencies from the runtime image. Only artifacts explicitly copied with `COPY --from=<stage>` appear in the final image. Everything else -- compilers, dev dependencies, test frameworks, source code -- is discarded.

## Why Multi-Stage?

| Concern | Single-stage | Multi-stage |
|---|---|---|
| Image size | 800MB+ (includes build tools) | 50-150MB (runtime only) |
| Attack surface | Large (compilers, package managers) | Minimal (only runtime deps) |
| Build secrets | Risk of leaking into layers | Contained in discarded stages |
| Build speed | Sequential | Parallel independent stages (BuildKit) |
| Dockerfile count | Multiple files for dev/prod | One Dockerfile, multiple targets |

## Basic Pattern: Builder + Runner

```dockerfile
# Stage 1: Build
FROM node:20-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN npm ci
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

# Stage 2: Production runtime
FROM node:20-alpine AS production
WORKDIR /app
ENV NODE_ENV=production
COPY --from=builder /app/package.json /app/package-lock.json ./
RUN npm ci --omit=dev
COPY --from=builder /app/dist ./dist
USER node
EXPOSE 3000
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=3s CMD wget -qO- http://localhost:3000/health || exit 1
CMD ["node", "dist/server.js"]
```

The builder stage contains the full source, dev dependencies, and build toolchain. The production stage copies only the compiled output and production dependencies.

## Go Application

Go compiles to a static binary, enabling scratch or distroless base images:

```dockerfile
# Build stage
FROM golang:1.22-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY go.mod go.sum ./
RUN go mod download
COPY . .
RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 GOOS=linux go build -ldflags='-w -s' -o /app/server ./cmd/server

# Minimal runtime -- from scratch (no OS, ~5MB final image)
FROM scratch
COPY --from=builder /app/server /server
COPY --from=builder /etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt /etc/ssl/certs/
EXPOSE 8080
ENTRYPOINT ["/server"]
```

The final image contains only the binary and CA certificates. No shell, no package manager, no attack surface.

## Python Application

```dockerfile
# Build stage with full toolchain
FROM python:3.12-slim AS builder
WORKDIR /app
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir poetry
COPY pyproject.toml poetry.lock ./
RUN poetry export -f requirements.txt --output requirements.txt --without-hashes
COPY . .
RUN poetry build --format wheel

# Runtime stage
FROM python:3.12-slim AS production
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=builder /app/requirements.txt ./
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt
COPY --from=builder /app/dist/*.whl ./
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir *.whl && rm *.whl
USER 1000
EXPOSE 8000
CMD ["gunicorn", "myapp:app", "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8000"]
```

## Rust Application

```dockerfile
# Build with full Rust toolchain
FROM rust:1.77-slim AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY Cargo.toml Cargo.lock ./
# Cache dependency compilation
RUN mkdir src && echo 'fn main(){}' > src/main.rs && cargo build --release && rm -rf src
COPY src ./src
RUN cargo build --release

# Distroless runtime (~20MB final image)
FROM gcr.io/distroless/cc-debian12
COPY --from=builder /app/target/release/myapp /
EXPOSE 8080
ENTRYPOINT ["/myapp"]
```

## Three-Stage Pattern: Build + Test + Production

```dockerfile
# Stage 1: Dependencies
FROM node:20-alpine AS deps
WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN npm ci

# Stage 2: Test
FROM deps AS test
COPY . .
RUN npm run lint && npm test

# Stage 3: Build
FROM deps AS builder
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

# Stage 4: Production
FROM node:20-alpine AS production
WORKDIR /app
ENV NODE_ENV=production
COPY --from=builder /app/dist ./dist
COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY --from=builder /app/package.json ./
USER node
CMD ["node", "dist/server.js"]
```

Build with `--target test` in CI to run tests. Build with `--target production` (or no target) for the final image. BuildKit runs the test and builder stages in parallel since they both depend on deps independently.

## Using --target for Different Environments

```bash
# Development image (includes dev tools, hot reload)
docker build --target deps -t myapp:dev .

# Test image (runs lint and tests)
docker build --target test -t myapp:test .

# Production image (minimal runtime)
docker build --target production -t myapp:latest .

# In docker-compose.yml:
services:
  app:
    build:
      context: .
      target: production
  app-dev:
    build:
      context: .
      target: deps
    volumes:
      - .:/app
    command: npm run dev
```

## Copying from External Images

`COPY --from` can reference named stages or external images:

```dockerfile
# Copy from an external image
COPY --from=nginx:1.25-alpine /etc/nginx/nginx.conf /etc/nginx/
COPY --from=busybox:1.36 /bin/wget /usr/local/bin/wget

# Copy from a named stage
COPY --from=builder /app/dist ./dist
```

This is useful for assembling images from multiple sources without building everything in one context.

## Size Comparison

| Application | Single-stage | Multi-stage | Reduction |
|---|---|---|---|
| Node.js web app | 1.2 GB | 180 MB | 85% |
| Go API server | 800 MB | 8 MB (scratch) | 99% |
| Python ML service | 3.5 GB | 450 MB | 87% |
| Rust CLI tool | 1.5 GB | 20 MB (distroless) | 99% |

## Best Practices

- Name every stage with `AS name` for readability and `--target` support
- Put the most-used stage last (it becomes the default build target)
- Use `scratch` for Go/Rust static binaries, `distroless` for languages needing libc
- Separate dependency installation from source copy for better caching in each stage
- Use `--target` in Compose for development vs production images from one Dockerfile
- Let BuildKit parallelize independent stages for faster builds

sections.title: Builder-runner pattern
sections.body: The most common multi-stage pattern separates a builder stage (with compilers, dev dependencies, source code) from a runner stage (with only runtime dependencies and compiled output). COPY --from=builder transfers artifacts between stages. The runner stage typically uses a smaller base image (alpine, slim, distroless, or scratch).

sections.title: Language-specific patterns
sections.body: Each language has an optimal multi-stage pattern. Node.js: separate npm ci from source copy, npm ci --omit=dev in production. Go: compile to static binary, use scratch. Python: export requirements from Poetry, pip install in slim image. Rust: cache dependency compilation with dummy main, use distroless. Java: build with Maven/Gradle, run with JRE-only image.

sections.title: Build-test-production pattern
sections.body: Add a test stage between build and production. The test stage runs linting, unit tests, and security checks. Use --target test in CI to fail fast on code issues. The production stage only runs if tests pass. BuildKit parallelizes independent stages for faster CI pipelines.

sections.title: Targeting environments with --target
sections.body: Use --target to select which stage to build. Development targets include dev tools and hot reload. Test targets run the test suite. Production targets contain only runtime artifacts. A single Dockerfile serves all environments. Compose uses build.target to select stages per service.

sections.title: Image size optimization
sections.body: Multi-stage builds reduce image size by 50-99%. Use scratch for static binaries (no OS layer). Use distroless for languages needing libc but no shell. Use alpine/slim for languages needing a package manager. Combine with .dockerignore and layer ordering from [[docker/images/build]] for maximum reduction.

scripts.name: Multi-Stage Build
scripts.description: Build a Docker image using a specific target stage from a multi-stage Dockerfile
scripts.inline: DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1 docker build --target "${TARGET_STAGE}" ${DOCKERFILE_PATH:+-f "$DOCKERFILE_PATH"} -t "${DOCKER_IMAGE}:${DOCKER_TAG}" --progress=plain "${BUILD_CONTEXT:-.}" && echo '' && echo "Built ${DOCKER_IMAGE}:${DOCKER_TAG} from stage '${TARGET_STAGE}'" && docker images "${DOCKER_IMAGE}:${DOCKER_TAG}" --format 'Size: {{.Size}}'

TARGET_STAGE.description: Target stage name (e.g. production, builder, test)

scripts.name: Compare Stage Sizes
scripts.description: Build multiple stages and compare their image sizes to verify multi-stage optimization
scripts.inline: echo '=== Building stages for size comparison ===' && echo '' && for STAGE in $(echo "$STAGES" | tr ',' ' '); do echo "Building stage: $STAGE" && DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1 docker build --target "$STAGE" -t "${DOCKER_IMAGE}:stage-${STAGE}" -q "${BUILD_CONTEXT:-.}" 2>/dev/null && echo "  $STAGE: $(docker images "${DOCKER_IMAGE}:stage-${STAGE}" --format '{{.Size}}')" && echo ''; done && echo '=== Size Summary ===' && docker images --filter "reference=${DOCKER_IMAGE}:stage-*" --format 'table {{.Tag}}\t{{.Size}}'

DOCKER_IMAGE.description: Image repository name for comparison builds

STAGES.description: Comma-separated stage names to compare (e.g. builder,test,production)

scripts.name: Generate Multi-Stage Dockerfile
scripts.description: Generate a multi-stage Dockerfile template for common language stacks
scripts.inline:
case "$LANGUAGE" in
  node|nodejs)
    cat <<'DOCKERFILE'
# Stage 1: Dependencies
FROM node:20-alpine AS deps
WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN npm ci

# Stage 2: Build
FROM deps AS builder
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

# Stage 3: Production
FROM node:20-alpine AS production
WORKDIR /app
ENV NODE_ENV=production
COPY --from=builder /app/dist ./dist
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN npm ci --omit=dev
USER node
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["node", "dist/server.js"]
DOCKERFILE
    ;;
  go|golang)
    cat <<'DOCKERFILE'
FROM golang:1.22-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY go.mod go.sum ./
RUN go mod download
COPY . .
RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 GOOS=linux go build -ldflags='-w -s' -o /app/server ./cmd/server

FROM scratch
COPY --from=builder /app/server /server
COPY --from=builder /etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt /etc/ssl/certs/
EXPOSE 8080
ENTRYPOINT ["/server"]
DOCKERFILE
    ;;
  python)
    cat <<'DOCKERFILE'
FROM python:3.12-slim AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt ./
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --prefix=/install -r requirements.txt
COPY . .

FROM python:3.12-slim AS production
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=builder /install /usr/local
COPY --from=builder /app .
USER 1000
EXPOSE 8000
CMD ["gunicorn", "myapp:app", "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8000"]
DOCKERFILE
    ;;
  rust)
    cat <<'DOCKERFILE'
FROM rust:1.77-slim AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY Cargo.toml Cargo.lock ./
RUN mkdir src && echo 'fn main(){}' > src/main.rs && cargo build --release && rm -rf src
COPY src ./src
RUN cargo build --release

FROM gcr.io/distroless/cc-debian12
COPY --from=builder /app/target/release/myapp /
EXPOSE 8080
ENTRYPOINT ["/myapp"]
DOCKERFILE
    ;;
  *)
    echo "Unsupported language: $LANGUAGE. Supported: node, go, python, rust" >&2
    exit 1
    ;;
esac

targetStage.description: Stage name to build (e.g. production, test, builder)

stages.description: Stage names to compare for size optimization

imageId.description: SHA256 digest of the built image
